GURRENT AFFAIRS,

One of the many rumours concerning the intentions
of the British Liberal Government with regard to Ire-
land is that the police force is to be handed over to the
control of the County Councils of Ireland. Now this
would be a real instalment of good, thorough-going re-
form. The police are, in some form or another, a use-
ful ‘and indispensable body. Of course, we have too
many of them in Ireland, though if the dutics of those we

- have were enlarged they might be made to do an amount

of useful work that would bear a reasonable relation to
their expense to the country.

It may be remembered that we trod on the toes of
some of our Nationalist Whole Hoggers on the question
of the Irish police. Some people denounce all Irishmen
who enter the force, and would have them held up to
national indignation. This. attitude has a certain
glamour for enthusiastic sentimentalists, whom, like
the poor, we will have always with us, but it is not
statesmanlike nor is it common sense. Under the circum-
stances, we think Irishmen do right to go under the

Government as civil servants and as * National ’’ school-
masters; we cannot sce sense in advocating that the
polica force—a body of some 12,000 men—should be boy-
cotbed by Irishmen, and so left undisputed ground for
the dumping of English and Scotch men.

We would wish that Irishmen kept out of the British
army and the British navy; but we have had too many
“ plantations ’ already to see our way to advocate that
a police force of 12,000 men should be utilised as a
machine for a new and a far-reaching “planta-
tion.””/ We do not know what proportion of
Irish policemen marry, but the percentage must be high.
If we assume that the average working life of a police-
man is thirty years, the handing over of the force to
Englishmen and Scotchmen by a boycott of it in Ireland
would mean that the seed, breed, and generation of the
number of those 12,000 who married would be added to
the foreign plantation in Ireland every thirty years.
The Anglicisation of Ireland would get a great fillip
from such a powerful pro-British movement as that.

Even constituted as they are at present, we would
prefer to see the R.I.0. manned by Irishmen rather
than by importations from Britain. If they are not of
any great use to the country, at least, they rear families
who may be; and from the families of policemen Ireland
has drawn very many of whom she has reason to be

proud.

If the police force were placed under the jurisdiction
of the County Councils all objection to the force from
the Nationalist point of view would cease, and the force
would probably become in the estimation of good citi-
zens one of the most popular in the country. Working
under the County Councils we would probably have a
civil police force that would have a wide sphere of use-
fulness. There is any amount of useful work that an
intelligent police force might be turnzd on to in Ireland ;
the collection of statistics and the detection of frauds
attempted against the industrial interest of Ireland by
endeavouring to sell foreign goods as Irish, are two
fields in which a capably and popularly governed police
force could do a vast amount of solid work.

We notice that there is a column headed  Litera-
ture ”’ in the “ Only National ’’ of Cork, of Saturday
iast. Amongst the various books, etc., reviewed is the
February number of the New Ireland Review. Most of
the notice of the latter publication—to be precise, 435
lines out of a total of 51—is devoted to a quotation
from the article on “Tom O’Kelly.””  The “ Only
National *’ says—* In a thoughtful review of a recent
story ‘ D.H.’ writes.”’—And then follows the long quo-
tation from the article. “ A recent story '’ is the “Ouly
National’s” nimble way of referring to “Tom O'Kelly.”
The quotation leaves off in the middle of a senteuce;
had the ‘“Only National’’ quoted a few lines further,
the name of the Editor of this paper would perforce
adorn the sombre, if at times somewhat ill-printed
columns, of our Bouthern atrocity !

The third annual meeting of the Cork Industrial De-
velopment Association was held last Friday. It is

_nearly six years now since we started the present

industrial movement, and we had long been calling, and
calling in vain, on the people concerned, and particu-,
larly the manufacturers, to fall into line and organise.
The Cork Association, if not exactly the first, anyhow
ropresents the first vigorous and business-like attempt
to tackle the industrial problem by means of more or
less popular organised effort.  Happily since then in
many. other centres, including: Dublin, organisab%ons

have been started; but as often, if not almost invari-
ably, happens, those who make the start retain the lead.
The Cork Industrial Association is still the leading entity
in the net-work of industrial organisations that are
working in various parts of Ireland.

In this land of raimeis it would be nothing short of a
miracle if a certain amount of that distinctive article
of Irish manufacture were not produced in connection
with such a now populaxr movement—popular, at least,
so far as talk goes—as that concerning Irish manufac-
ture. For instance, the Lord Mayor of Cork said:—
“They could get everything they wanted in Ireland.
He didn’t-care how ornamental the article might be, or
how beautiful it might be, anything that was made in
Ireland should be beautiful enough for an Irishman to
look at.”” Wae take it from this that the tea which we
assume his lordship drinks is grown somewhere in the
neighbourhood of Sundays Well. We sometimes have
to laugh at our art-critics, but his lordship’s views on
beauty will scarcely meet with unanimous acceptance.

It is significant to find that even Brother Goulding
as Chairman of the Great Sick and Sourface Railway is
coming within the influence of the Cork Industrial As-
sociation. In the course of the report we read:—* The
Irish Cattle Traders’ and Stockowners’ Association com-
plained to us of the excessive railway rates charged on
skins sent from Kilkenny to Cork, whilst a particularly
low rate was charged for the transit of this article from
that town to Liverpool via Waterford. We brought
this matter under the notice of Sir William J. Goulding,
the Chairman of the G. S. and W. Railway Company,
who met our complaint in a most courteous and business-
like manner, and considerably reduced the former rate.’”’
The proceedings, as reported, were generally practical
and business-like, and the Cork Industrial Association
has earned the unstinted congratulations of the country
for its work.

Brother Knight (we don’t know how the k got there),
County Grand Master of the Monaghan Orangemen, is
concerned about the forthcoming marriage of the King
of Spain. According to a newspaper report, the child
of light declared:—“ It was a marriage which would
connect Protestant England with Roman Catholic Spain
(hisses). It was a marriage which he, as a member of
the Orange Institution, did not like to hear of, and it
was a bad day for England when she was going to sur-
render one of her princesses to Roman Catholic Spain
(hear, hear). They had learned in the past that the
Bible was the secret of England’s greatness (cheers).
Were they going to give it up? It would be a bad day
for her, and he hoped the loyalists would speak out and
proclaim that it was contrary to the wishes of the
Protestants that the marriage should take place. It
was, he said, a question whether England was to re-
main Protestant in the future as she had done in the
past. God alone, he said, could only tell what the
future held, and he advised all Protestants to stick
to their principles and let their watch word be ‘No
surrender * and keep their powder dry.””’

Under the heading “ Tuam Notes’’ we read in a
Western contemporary :— Contemplating now what
might have been is no practical value. The barge of
time has glided beyond our reach in the direction of
judgment’s dawn, and no act, nor word, nor prayer of
ours can alter the happening of the past.”” Further
down in the course of these “ Tuam Notes,”” and hefore
we have had time to properly get over “the barge of
time,”” wo read:— You cannot look through the dio-
ramma of the future, nor take down from the casel of
the past the deeds which are indelibly recorded there-
on; but we can stand in readiness to meet the exi-
gencies of the present, to show that our hearts are still
yearning for an Irish Parliament in College Green.’’
It would go hard even with Mr. Puck in his sprightliest
humour to better these clippings from the deadly
serious writer of “Tuam Notes.”

Poor Lord Bung'’s Daily Ezpress is very much pre-
turbed concerning the attitude of the British Govern-

THE LEADER.

MarcHE 3, 1906.

ment towards the teaching of the Irish language in the
“ National ”’ Schools. It commences an edlf.orm'
articlo in its last Friday’s issue in this way:—*The
preciso intentions of the Government with x‘e{ex‘enw' to
the rostoration of the bounty-fed system of stimulating
the study of Irish in National Schools remain an un-
known quantity. The questions asked yesterday by Mr.
Lonsdale were sufficiently definite in form to have
afforded Mr. Bryce, if he desired to be candid, an excel-
lent opportunity of stating plainly how far the Govern-
ment proposes to go in the direction of bribing the
Gaelic League to maintain a policy of friendly silence.”’

Poor Lord Bung’s Bzpress is to be pitied. As the
Ezpress young man writes along he gets his blood up.
‘“ Teachers who care little,”” he says, “ and sometimes
knew less about the language succumbed rapidly to the
combined influence of Gaelic League dictation on the
one hand and Treasury largess on the other. The
number of pupils wasting their time in studying the
clementary portions of a difficult and a dead language
increased by tens of thousands every year. The grant
for teaching rose in the same proportion, and by the
time that Mr. Long arrived on the scene, and deter-
mined to put an end to the farce, the Government en-
dowment of the Gaelic League amounted to £12,000.”

Lord Bung’s Fazpress should, without delay, get Lord
Ashtown to write a letter enlightening the Big Brother
on the perilous situation which the bitter anti-Irish
people for whom it speaks have to face. If not, even
the editor of the Bzpress may have to learn the alphabet
of “a difficult and dead language’’ yet. It is easily
seen by the inflated language in which verbosity mas-
querades as strength that the Hzpress feels how im-
potent is its rage. Even alliterative flights like “peda-
gogic purveyors of patriotism ’’ do not disguise the con-
scious weakness of the Fapress that it would fain hide.

We read:—“ It is not improbable that the new grant
will be arranged on a basis substantially different from
that on which the old grant rested, and that it will
yield proportionately richer results to the pedagogic
purveyors of patriotism of the Gaelic League brand.”

Mr. Marcus Goodbody, replying to the toast of “ The
Industries of Ireland * at the dinner of the Mercantile
Association, commented strongly on what he aptly
called “The Amateur Industrialists.”’ He said:—“ He
would urge on them to avoid the amateur industrialists,
with their stalls run by titled ladies, and selling goods
sometimes too dear and sometimes too cheap.  That
made a farce of the whole thing, he thought, and
he was often astonished at seeing hard-headed business
men encouraging them.”’

What is the value of a resolution? At a recent meet-
ing of the Westport Board of Guardians, one of the
matters disposed of was the election of a nurse. On
the minutes of the Council there is a record of a resolu-
tion that as regards all appointments a preference be
given to candidates who have a knowledge of Irish.
The vacancy for a nurse was duly advertised, but there
was no mention of the Irish language. Mr, P. O’Don-
nc_ll, stated, “ We have in this Union one of the largest
Irish-speaking populations, and in this house there are
unfortunate people, who, if it were to save their lives,
could not ask for a drink of water in English.”  And
yot, notwithstanding this state of affairs, and the reso-
lution on the minutes, there was no reference to Irich
in the advertisement. There were only two candidatcs
for the post, and both were from the locality. One of
tho candidates it appears is an Irish speaker, and when
a guardian asked Myr. Dorris who proposed the non-Irish
speaker—Mr. Dorris, by the way, is also a member of
the Gaelic League—when a guardian asked this Gaclic
Lc&guer “‘what about the Irish language?’ Mr. Dorris
replied—* There is nothing about the Tvish language in
the advertisemont, and let me say about the Irish lan-
guage that perhaps the question is put a little too far
by Mr. O’Donnell. You could go down to Achill and
get a lot of Achill jumpers who speak Irish.”” Be that

e

e g

" Marcy 3, 1906.

as 1t may, what had Achill jumpers to do with the
election of a nurse? The candidato who was an Irish-
speaker was not a jumper anyway. As far as we can
Jjudge from the report, there was no contention that
the Irish-speaking nurse was not fully competent. The
non-Irish speaking nurse was clected by 37 votes to 17.

Hercules Edward Rowley prosided at the recent an-
nual meeting of the notorious ‘“ Irish Church Missions '’
which was held at Belfast. In the course of Iercules’
address, he said :— From the last annual report it ap-
peared that the itinerant ovangelists held 241 lantern
meetings, attended by 11,000 Protestants and 6,000
Roman Catholics.”” How well they were ablo to count
them!  Hercules Edward Rowley is better known as
Lord Langford. ¥

At the same meeting a Rev. R. E. Waters said:—
“ Recently he had had a letter from Dr. Long, and in
it the writer stated that he gave a Testament to a
Roman Catholic woman in his dispensary, and when she
returned she said she could not get reading it because
her husband (who was also a Roman Catholic) took it
to his work, and every spare moment was reading it.
She got another Testament for herself. There were
4.899 attendances last year at the dispen: in Lime-
rick, and to everyone coming there the Word of God
was preached. Dr. Long’s work was done not only to
shake men from Rome, but to win them to the Lord
Jesus Christ.”

There was a curious mustard prosecution at Navan
Petty Sessions recently. Mr. James Owens of that town
was prosecuted for having sold as Colman’s mustard
(a British make) an article adulterated with foreign
matter. Mr. Gerald Byrne, Dublin, appeared for the
defendant and stated that the British mustard makers
made exhaustive enquiries, and ascertained that a con-
diment which they supplied largely in England, and
contained ingredients added for the purpose of making
it more palatable, was sent in mistake to Mr. Owens.
The error was discovered on analysis, and the firm ad-
mitted that this quality of mustard should not have
been supplied to Mr. Owens. Under the circumstances
the magistrates, knowing Mr. Owens to be a respectable
trader, and the police assenting, Mr. Gerald Byrne
asked that a nominal fine only be inflicted.

Mr. Byrne on behalf of Mr. Owens pleaded guilty,
and Mr. Owens was fined 2s. 6d. The startling sequel
to all this is contained in revelations subsequently made
by Mr. Owens in a letter to a local paper, the Meath
Chronicle.

According to Mr. Owens, Mr. Gerald Byrne of Dub-
lin, “ Kindly undertook my defence free of charge.” Is
Mr, Gerald Byrne a philanthropist then, or a solicitor?
Mr. Owens is not enamoured of the advocacy that he
got free, gratis and for nothing, for he refers to him-
self as being “in the false position in which I am at
present placed, having a solicitor pleading guilty for
me for an offence which I never committed,””  Mr.

- Owens states that Mr. Gerald Byrne was not his solicitor

by choice, he came and took up the case. Mr. Owens
says:—“If I were left to myself I would have em-
ployed a Navan solicitor, and I would not, I am sure,
appear before the public in the false position in which
I appear at present. The real offenders wore Messis.

. Colman and Co., whose name Mr, Byrne did not men-

tion throughout the whole proceedings. Not alone did
Mr. Byrne come to Navan and undertake to defend the
case, but when I was convicted and fined he paid the
fine, etc., himself. The case is over now, and I am
sorry to say I have been made a fool of.””  This reveals
a most extraordinary state of affairs indeed, and per-
haps Mr. Gerald Byrne would throw some further light
on it,

What does Mr. Bung intend to do on the 17th Maxch?
Does he intend to sponge on St. Patrick. His Graco the
Archbishop of Dublin refers in his Lenten Pastoral to
the gpongers on St. Patrick. He says:— The gratify-

THE LEADER. LIS

ing observance of the festival of our National Apostle as
a general holiday is still attended with one serious draw-
back. In certain districts, not a few of the houses
licensed for the sale of intoxicating drink are kept open
on that day.”” THis Graco further on refers directly to
thoso lower-class Bungs—for some Bungs are even worse
than other Bungs—who sponge on St. Patrick. They
did nothing to establish the holiday, but still, like the
bad Irish landlords, though they have not sown they
are anxious to reap. Iis Grace says:— It is necessary
to repeat that tho liconsed houses that are kept open on
St. Patrick’s Day, are, as a rule, of the lower and less
reputable class.”” His Grace also says:—‘ A vigorous
combined effort should continue to be made by the
Clergy to secure a general closing of licensed houses on
St. Patrick’s Day.”

There was a ‘“ God Save the King ’’ concert one night
last week at Dungannon. It was given in “ St. George’s
Hall ”* by the “ Dungannon Choral Society.”” Needless
to say there was no song in Irish on the programme.
Being a “ God Save the King '’ Society, most of the
members are “ Saved,”” but a few ““ Idolators ” joined it
in 1904 and took part in the chanting of the tune that
really means for the most part “ Damn the Idolators.””
A larger number of ““ Idolators’’ joined this year. We
hear that Herr Werner, the conductor, being an “Idola-
tor,”” some of the “Saved ’’ objected, but the objection
was over-ruled. We hear also that some Irish Irelanders
were amongst the audience at this “ God Save the King’’
concert.

Father Matt Ryan of Knockavilla, Co. Tipperary, has
shown what one man can do for the spread of the Irish
language. Four years ago he advertised for a native
Trish speaker as principal teacher in the M.N.S. of Dun-
askea. ITe succeeded in getting what he wanted in the
person of Mr. Buckley from Ballyvourney. Father Matt
showed a good example by studying Irish himself, and
to-day Irish is taught in every school in his parish.
Mr. Buckley left Knockavilla for a larger school down
South, and Mr. O’Sullivan a native Irish speaker from
Kerry has replaced him. Mr. Kiely, one of the Gaelic
League organisers, tells us:—* Along with teaching the
National Board Programme, Prayers and Songs are
taught in Irish; Dialogues and Irish Plays are prepared
and staged, and all this has been done without the
least set back to the English Programme work. No
school in the parish has got lower than the mark ‘Good’
from the Board’s Inspector, the majority have got V.
Good ’ and some few Excellent’ this year and last.
I went through the schools last week, and really T
found some of the children almost as good as native
speakers. It shows that it is not the bare requirements
of the National Board that are taught; the subject is
taught with the one object in view, and that is to make
Irish the living spoken language of the rising generation”

We congratulate Mr. Hazleton on being selected for
the North Galway constituency. It would be a good
thing for the Irish Parliamentary Party if it included
twenby or thirty men of Mr. Hazleton’s type. The
Trish Party, though it contains many individual mem-
bers in sympathy with the new school, is in the main
somewhat out of touch with the Trish Ireland movement. -
Tor its own sake, as well as for that of the country, an
infusion of new blood is much to be desired, and pro-
bably but for Mr. William O’Brien’s antics, and the
consequent oxtra special straining after unity, many new
and more up-to-date men would have found their way
into the new Party.

Tho staff of the Simply Deplorable, Ltd., consumed
a dinner last Saturday; it was almost a wonder that
they did not “prove their tolerance’’ by having the
dinnor on a Friday, so that the “Saved’’ could have
all the good things to themselves. We note that Mr.
Soxton was conspicuous by his absence from the spread.
The menu is duly advertised in the news columns of
the Simply Deplorable.  There are, we understand,
some poor people in Ireland who sometimes advertise
the fact that they had a goose for dinner on some festive

TIre

occasion by giving appropriate bones to the: youngsters
of the house to play with in the street, and under the
observation of the neighbours.  The Stmply Deplorable
sets out its menu by way, perhaps, of making the
mouths of the staff of 4lf Fow water! They had cham-
‘pagne and turtle soup, and “roast Surrcy fowls.” Trish-
reared fowls were not good enough, we suppose, for the
patriotic staff of the Simply Deplorable! We aro told
that the menu card was very artistic, and that there
was a round tower on it; what a pity the artist did
not represent a boycotted Irish fowl shedding its feathers
in disgust at the Simply Deplorable. We see no men-
tion of the puffed “ Spring Chicken '’ on the menu card.
One James James sang ‘“ Hen wlad fy Nhadan,”” Taffy's
National Anthem; but we see no mention of a song in
Tvish.

Mr. Robert Donovan, of the “ God-Save-the-King
British Institute of Journalists, proposed the toast of
“Ireland a Nation’ in a truly simply deplorable speech.
They are “ proving their tolerance’’ feverishly on the
Freeman staff just now it would appear. Mr. Donovan
remarked : —

It was a toast which not long ago might bo regarded as having
somewhat of a party complexion, but he thought all would agree
with him—and he was glad to say that the Freeman Staff repr
sented all scetions of the Irish peoplo (hear, hear)—all would agre=
with him when he said that Irishmen were beginning to recognise
that this toast had no party meoning, but that it oxpressed tha
feelings and convictions which were as much a part and parcel of,
should he say, the Orangeman’s faith as it was of the Nationalist’s
(hear, bear).

The man Coote, of Carnthall, and his like are getting
some curious testimonials these times!

Mr. Donovan went on:—

The toast he had to give them was the toast of Ireland a Nation
(applause). There were great differonces of opinion as to how
much this toast might be made to imply—he meant, upon questions
of the machinery that was necessary to Ireland as a Nation to
realise itself.

We wonder now is the “ Spring Chicken’’ or “ Zaza '’
an ingredient of “ Ireland a Nation!’’ and does Ireland
increase in prosperity by the Freeman staff eating
“roast Surrey fowls?’

Why this constant, half-slave protesting, particularly
in face of the traditional anti-Irish attitude of the
Sourfaces, of tolerance? Were the Irish ever intolerant?
Mr. Donovan, in a rather weak style of raimeis,
declared : —

For him-—and he thought he might say for thom—they aoccapted
no narrower definition than that which the Protestant apostle of
Irish Nationality, Thomas Davis, gavo (applause). They be-
lieved in a Nationality that, as he said, must represent and contain
ail the races of Ircland; it must not bo Coltie, it must not be
Saxon, it must be Irish (cheers). It musst comprehend the
eloudy and lightning genius of tho Gael, tho placid strength of the
Sassenach, the martialling insight of thoe Norman, and, if he might
attribute the long age, the imporishablo vitality, the indestruc-
tible prosperity of the institution with which thoy were associated
to any one fact, it would be to this, that from the boginning until
now, with very brief lapses, it had reprosented tho broad conception
of Davis with rogard to the meaning of Irish Nationalily (applause).

LEADER.

MAaRrcH 3, 1908.

The peroration of Mr. Donovan of the Simply De-
plorable, and of the Irish tail of the “ God-Save-the-
King ”’ British Institute of Journalists, was in the
best style of Mr. High Faluter.—

And, therofore, it was that ho felt that in giving them, the staff’
of what was called the National newspapor, this toast of “Ircland
a Nation,” ho was giving it to an assembly that was representative
of the wholo peoplo—Protestant and Catholic, Conservative and
Whig and Nationalist--and their tolerance, their comradeship, their
wood-followship, their loyalty to ono another was only representative
of that genuino patriotism that rose above all party, and was
fortified, inspired, and strengthencd by loyally to the principle of
Nationhood (loud cheers).

So Conservatives and Whigs who may throaten to line
the ditches if we are even  devolutionised ’’ from ’appy
Hongland, are part and parcel of “Ireland a Nation.”
Vive raimeis-—long live the Simply Deplorable!

After Mr. Donovan, the Lord Mayor. Mr. Nannetti,
declared he was “unmanned,’”’ whatever that is, by the
reception accorded to his unworthy self by the staff of
the Simply Deplorable, Ltd. Mr. Nannetti, in the
course of his speech, proceeded to give an exhibition of
that half-slave fawning on a section of the country
that, as a body, are of England’s Faithful Garrison—
the half-slave spirit that makes one often feel that we
deserve a lot of the kicks that we get. Said Mr. Nan-

netti:—

But who wero the men they thought most about in their Irish

Party ? They were tho Protestants like Captain Donelan
(applausc), Swift MacNeill—another colleague, he might say, of
the Freeman—mon like tho-e, who, by their voice and inspiration,
would teach them how to make Ireland what she ought to be, and
would bo, he hoped, very soon (hear, hear).
So then Mr. MacNeill and Captain Donelan, two
“Saved "’ of the Party, are thought more of than John
Redmond, John Dillon, or any of the other Papists!
Perhaps if Mr. Nannetti became a jumper they might
have made him Chairman of the Party in order to
“prove their tolerance.” A Mr. Porter, on rising to
speak, was received by the strains of a British public-
house lyric by the name of “ For He's a Jolly Good
Fellow.”” The proceedings did not wind up with the
toasts of * The Spring Chicken '’ and the Surrey fowl.

We hear that at a recent preliminary examination for
service in the Bank of Ireland, a young candidate was
asked who nominated him. The candidate gave the in-
formation, and the comment was “ A Catholic, I be-
lieve.”” He was then asked where he had been cduca’ed.

ART-TASTERS

HERE are tea-tasters and butter-tasters; there
are also art-tasters. The former follow useful
avocations by which they earn an honest living; the
latter are often, if not usually, gentlemen—we are too
gallant to include ladies—who have more time on their
hands than brains. Oh, Art—with a capital A—what
drivel is written and spoken in thy name! We parti-
cularly refer to the painters’ art. In the literary field
the intelligent and normal multitude who read books
are freely permitted to have their views on works that
are literature, or that purport to be literature; it is
a like case in connection with dramatic art, but when
you come to painting it is a different matter altogether.
In some country places visitors are warned to “ beware_
of the dog;’’ in picture galleries there is an unwritten
warning—beware of the art-critic.. We put it direct
to the secret and innermost recesses of our readers’ con-
sciousness—are they not in dread of saying plainly and
bluntly aloud—that is a good picture, or that is a bad
picture, for fear that he with the shaggy beard, or he
with the long hair, or he with a bald patch and a
solemn face, might pierce him to the heart with a look
of scorn? The poor man would freely express his
honest likes and dislikes, his appreciation, or want of it,
for literary art, but the unwritten law, “beware of the
critic,”” strikes him dumb before a picture. The fear
of focussing the scornful eyes of a pack of art-tasters is
too much for him. The comic element in the situation
is that each of the dumfounding art-tasters is prob-
ably convinced of nothing more strongly than that the
other tasters don’t know what they are talking about
when they speak or write of Art. However, they in-
variably sink their differences, and join in a unanimous
howl against any rash outsider—who can talk freely
and intelligently of Dante, Sha,kespcarel, Cervantes,
Thackeray, and other literary masters—if he should
say anything about a painted picture. The art-tasters
form a gang or combine, and say—* Out, Philistine,
out|”

To the understanding mind there is something
exquisitely ontertaining in watching a gang of
art-tasters  disputing  solemnly, seriously,  some-
times hotly, the question of fact whether a certain work
of Art ig genuine, or only an imitation. A jury of dog-
fanciers who could not decide as to whether a ocertain
animal was a grey-hound or an Irish terrier, would not

22

be regarded by an intelligent autsiderias reliable-experts
in the dog line. Is it any wonder an intelligent public
laugh when they observe a gang of Art fanciers who
assure the world that they know all tha is: worth. know-
ing about Art (capital A, and pronounced “ Awb "') and
- yet cannot agree whether a given picturc is a “greab
master *’ or a great fraud? .

If the public were to take the art-tasters seriously,
they (the art-tasters)—at least, such of them as cver
painted anything—might be allowed to make a living
by buying and selling one another’s pictures; for they
apparently agree that as regards “ Awt’' the public is
an ass. What is the poor P.P. who wants to ornament
his Church with painting to do? He daren’t have an
opinion of his own, for the art-tasters would yell in
chorus—what does he know about ‘“ Awt%’ let him
keep to theology. If he should call in an nrt-lx!sl-cr.hc
will, no doubt, get advice which may sooth his mind
until & second art-taster happens to come along. What
is the poor P.P. to think then? No. 2 art-taster will
probably lift up his eyes, and assure the P.P. that No.
1 knows absolutely nothing about “ Awt!"”

Picture one hundred wealthy men of affairs, cultured
in a manner natural to their important spheres of use-
fulnessin the national and social economy, having made
up their minds to spend each a thousand pounds on pic-
tures for the adornment of their houses. Poor men,
what are they to buy? Fearful of the dreadful {yranny
of the art-tasters, who might launch “ Philistine ”’ at
their heads if they paid their money for what they
fancied in the way of pictures, they completely lose
their self-reliance; they allow themselves to be led by
some art-taster, and one of them having bought a pic-
ture a little mild controversy springs up around it. Is
it genuine? Is it by Mrv. Honest, or is it one of Mr
Fake’s? Nay, is it “ Awt '’ at all? If the good man
saw a picture that pleased him, and went and bought it,
some critic—who could not answer in the affirmative if
he were asked if he had ever got five pounds for a pic-
ture painted by himself—would assure the poor man
that he knew nothing whatever about “ Awt,”” and that
his purchase plainly indicated that he was a “ Philis-
tine.”

The art-tasters in Ireland are a comical lot.  Wig-
wag, wig-wag, go their tongues about “ Awt,”” « Awt,”
“Awt;’’ each assuring the world that those of the other
coterie don’t know what they are talking about. Bald-
spot says to Longhair—*“T hear that that Roundhat is
a rising artist.”’  Longhair shakes his mane, and ex-
claims—“ Why, Roundhat cawn’t draw!”

However, the art-tasters do not draw large audiences ;
they talk mostly to themselves, and the prudent news-
paper manager never thinks of putting on an extra ten
thousand to his printing order on the strength of a pro-
posed article on “ Awt.”” The prudent editor likew'se
would count ten before he took a long series of “ Awt ’’
articles; he might be wanting to avoid facetious post-
cards from “Philistine’’ readers wanting to know
“ when is ‘ Awt’ going to stop?”’

After all, the Art-sense of a people is manifested by
what people want in the way of Art. If they show a
sad lack of taste there is something radically wrong;
something so radically wrong that it cannot be cured
by coteries caterwauling about “ Awt,”” and, whilst
they cannot agree amongst themselves, terrorising
simple, but honest, people out of their reliance in their
own judgment when they go to look at a picture, or,
mayhap, intend to buy it.

Dealing in pictures is rather like dealing in copies of
first editions of popular books, or dealing in rare postage
stamps. A work of Dickens is of a certain value as
literature; it is of the same literary value in a modern
sixpenny edition as in a first edition for which some col-
lecting faddist may pay £200. The market for pictures
is somewhat like the market in first editions of famous
books.  There is a sort of rare old postage stamp
market made in them that have not necessarily any re-
lation to their intrinsic value to the purchaser, If a

man paid £1,000 for some volumes of Shakespeare’s
works, and did not read them, or read them merely
as a task in order to be able to say that he had read
them, but failed to appreciate their intrinsic worth,

with more money than brains is induced, by some
fashionable Art tyranny, to pay £1,000 for a piece of
canvas that is to him a piece of canvas, and nothing
ro?

mos? course, the Intermediate, the *“ National ”’ Board
of Education, and the lack of University facilities
for the Irish people have all combined to dwarf, if not
pervert, the artistic taste .nf ]1'91@11(!. T};e wise
mumbling of art-tasters has l}LL)e utility, even if it hag
not a disutility, in the circumstances. The best
thing, perhaps, that the art-tasters could (lg at present,
is to keep their tongues quiet, and put their fountain-

MR. DAYITT ON CARDINALS MORAN AND
GIBBONS_AND THE SECULAR SCHOOLS.—IY.

N his reply to the Bishop of Limerick Mr. Davitt
I wanted to show that the banishing of religious
teaching from schools would not destroy, would rather
improve the religious faith of the people. He offered
in proof the fact that the Catholic Church is progressing
most in America and Australia, where the schools are
socularised.  And he introduces the name of Cardinal
Micran and Cardinal Gibbons in connection with it. A

Catholic Democrat ”’ corrected him for identifying
those names with the public school system; and M.
Davitt at once denied that he did in any way suggest
that the two Cardinals approved the public school sys-
tem. He is quite right, as we find after closely examin-
ing his words. But his loose way of expression certainly
left the impression on me, and if he did not want to leave
that impression, what could b2 his reason for introduc-
ing their names at all? But his escaping from that
does not secure his escape. He, at any rate, connects
the progress of Catholicity in America and Australia
with the existence in those countries of a system of edu-
cation which has banished religious teaching from the
schoals.  Mr. Davitt forgot to tell us, however, where
are the Catholic children educated in those countries.
Is it in the public schools? Some are, I know;:
for Catholics cannot have schools everywhere; and if
the two Cardinals, whose names he wrenches into a sen-
tence for no clear purpose unless that purpose which he
afterwards repudiated, if the other bishops, priests, and
Catholic people of those countries thought that the Ca-
tholic faith could flourish or even subsist under the
influence of the public school system, I should like to
see how Mr. Davitt would explain their action in the
matter. The fact is that, whilst they have to pay their
share of the taxes to support the public schools, they
have, all over the two countries, at their own private
cost built, equipped, and organised a system of Catholic
schools. Now, it is curious that they would go to that
enormous expense if they were of Mr. Davitt’s mind
on the public school system. Mur. Davitt will have to
explain that simple fact before he can make headway.
Perhaps Mr, Davitt understands their case better than
they do themsclves? If he pretends to that, he will
huvle to tell us how that can be—another rock in his
path.

The plain fact is—the Catholics of America and Aus-
tralia have made those enormous sacrifices because they
believe that the Catholic faith of their children would
finally be undermined by the public school system. That
seems quite plain to my common sense; otherwise why
should they spend the money on Catholic schools?

Ah! but, says Mr. Davitt, how do you explain the
fact that the public school system is the one that pre-
vails in America and Anstralia, and yet Catholicity pro-
gresses thero? T explain it, Mr. Davitt, by a fact which

you unfairly kept in the background-—namely, that the
Catholic children are mostly educated in Catholic schools.
MY, Davitt loft us to infer—a, piece of sharp practice—
that the Catholic children of America and Australia are
!nust;lyl; if not entirely, educated in the public schools.
That is Mr. Davitt’s method of: leading the public how
to find the truth.

Is Mr. Davitt aware that considerably over 1,000,000
children attend the Catholic primary schools of the

The *“Catholic Democrat’’ reminds him that the

'The ““ Catholic Democrat > doubled him up by his reply

Perhaps Mr. Davitt is likewise unaware that public

rthe side of a question opposed to his own. Then, the

lics have even been charged with being enemices of the
-people and the flag. Any careful observer in the city

although a Protestant of the firmest kind, T believe the

-time has come to recognise this fact, and for us to lay
aside prejudices and patriotically meet this question.””

Just a twelvemonth ago, the Augusta (Ga.) Chronicle

the Indian, and fitting him in a practical way for civi-

Tn the North American Review of Decembor, 1880, Mr.

And he adds :—“ Do not tell me that this would have

I could quote many other Americans—neither Catho-

France the result of Mr. Davitt’s system of education in

Even as to mere mental training, those secularised
schools have proved themselves to be very much a fraud.
It is a fact well known that as soon as Catholic schools
were allowed in France, they attracted the children even
of anti-clericals and infidels, from the State schools ; and
the plan of the Secularists for competing with them was
not to improve efficiency, but to increase prizes and
burses, paid out of the public taxes to bribe the children
into the State schools.

In the article in the North American Review, from
which I have already quoted, Mr. White gives the evi-
dence of inspectors and others as to the inefficiency of
the public schools in the United States. According to
the New York Herald (of June 16th, 1903), 1,000 candi-

School of New York; 305 received the required marks
to qualify for admission. Of those, 17 took such marks
as to qualify as honour students. Now there were 25
public schools competing, and 13 Catholic schools. Yet
of the 17 honour pupils, 8 were from the public schools
and 6 from the Catholic schools.  Three candidates
scored 98 per cent., and those three were from the Catho-
lic schools. Those candidates were all girls; and nuns
had the training of the Catholic girls. T have by me a

- letter which appeared in the New York Sun, in Novem-

ber, 1903, from one who signs himself “ A Protestant
Business Man.”  Tn it he says that boys from Catholic
schools are preferable for office work to those from the
public schools; and for that reason nearly all the boys
in his office are from Catholic schools. He says he finds
that boys are befter grounded in reading, writing, and

arithmetic in the Catholic schools. I can lot Mr. Davitt
_have more.of this, if he wants it. The Catholic schools
. in England, according to the reports of F.M. Inspectors,
_ are, in spite of their poverty, nock and neck with the
_ board schools, and they beat the Anglican schools. T

g I S HINGS are turning out better than might have been

expected. The Liberal Government seem to have
realised that what is needed by Ireland is not govern-
ment for Ivishmen, a thing impossible, but government
&y Ivishwmen. It remains to see how they will put these
ideas in practice. The one unfortunate feature in the
whole affair seems to me the pretence of Ulster’s con-
version. I do not believe the assertion of an argument
s0 dishonest can in the long run do any good. I am
brought perpetually in contact with a large number of
educated Protestant Ulstermen and I know there is but
the faintest justification for any such alleged change of
attitude. We have two strong points in our case; Ire-
land’s crimelessness and Ireland’s decay. For the fact
that we have a lower birthrate than France with all its
immoralities, is surely not a matter for jest. It is better
then to base our case on bed-rock facts such as these,
and seek to solve the East Ulster difficulty rather than
to deny it.

We have as yet no sufficient materials before us to
enable us to ascertain the intentions of the Liberal Go-
vernment. For “the association of Irishmen with the
conduct of their affairs’’ enunciates a principle, but
not its application. We only know that whatever are
the individual opinions of Mr. Bryce, the Government,
as a whole, seem committed not to introduce a measure
of Home Rule equally extensive with that of Gladstone.
We are, therefore, only to have select scenes from Home
Rule. The question for us is consequently what parts
of the play are going to be left out. Are they going to
omit the part of Hamlet, or will they merely cut out
the players? Hamlet I would identify with the Chief
Secretary, and the person who controls the Treasury ;
melancholy or madness has hitherto been his portion ;
the players seem to me adequately represented by those,
our spokesmen, who propose to perform on the floor of
the Bank in College Green, and whose function it would
be to sustain the sensational and moderately useful
office of pricking the conscience of the King.

Whenever ‘the Government promulgate their pro-
posals of compromise, we must keep clearly before our
minds the distinction I have frequently alluded to in
these columns between administrative power and legisla-
tive power, ruling and law-making, or in still plainer
language, the Government and the Parliament. The
two things are so mixed up in the English constitutional
system that we find it hard to disentangle them. But
Jet us remember that Roosevelt is the ruler of America,
and Wyndham and Long were tho rulers of Ircland.
Yet Roosevelt could not pass a law, or compel the Senate
to accept his treaties. Wyndham and Long could pass
no law if the Irish Party objected. Bryce, having the
control of the administration, is enabled to nullify the
law under which Gaelic Leaguers were thrown into
#aol for using their Irish names. The law itself was
the creature not of the Parliament, but of a projudiced
judiciary appointed by a corrupt and irresponsible
Government administration again. 5

Grattan, on the other hand, could pass a law through
Parliament easily enough, yet he had not the smallest

say in the government of Ireland. And it was this vory
thing that brought about the destruction of the Irish

Now it is of supreme importance for Irishmen and
Trish members to keep this distinction between the
Government and the Parliament in their minds, and to
recognise the overwhelming importance of the former.
The Liberals intend to give us something less than
Home Rule. Which are we then to abandon—the
powers of a responsible Chief Secretary, or the powers
such as oxisted in Grattan’s time of a responsible Par-
liament? The latter is what was recently conceded to
the Transvaal, and it has been found so unsatisfactory

Tt is this Tsar conceded to Russia in

control over the executive, becomes a national academy
of oratory, and little more, especially if, as is probable

ceded. For bounties were the one strong point of
Grattan’s Parliament. There would, no doubt, be
grand debates on the floor of the Bank. Orators would
begin in Irish, relapsing after a while into eloquent
veupta.  Whenever a patriot accepted a job from the

a la Flood and Grattan, which would fill columns of

Now there are unfortunately a good many things
which make the floor-of-the-Bank idea more attractive

naturally of College Green.

lics spouting would work wonders. In both cases there

WANTED—AN IRISH INSURANCE CO.—IIL
By AN INSURANCE MANAGER.

OME of the money, in the form of premiums on
insurance policies, that now flows unceasingly

every week from Ireland to England and Scotland for
investment by English and Scottish directors, would
soon be diverted into an Irish insurance company, if
we had one, and find lucrative investment in other en-
terprises, giving employment to the people throughout
Ireland, and helping to keep them at home. As the law
stands, companies doing business here at present are
under no obligation to invest any of their funds in this

country, and judging by the cackling of one or two of ,

them, a few years ago, over the fact that they had lent
a small sum to an Irish Corporation, we may be quite
sure that such an occurrence is very, very rare. There can
be but little doubt that, if two or three earnest men of
well-known business ability and integrity could be got
together, and agree to act as directors or trustees of an
Irish mutual insurance company, the Irish banks would
heartily support them.

Bank managers are amongst the most successful in-
surance agents for existing (foreign) companies, but
with a good Irish company to work for, they would have
the satisfaction of using their energies for, instead of
against, their own country. I say “against’’ ad-
visedly ; for; absenteeism has been the chief factor in
ruining Ireland. Properly governed countries—such as
New Zealand—impose an extra tax of 20 per cent. on
absentees. In Ireland, however, as if it were not bad
enough to have an absentee Parliament and absentee
landlords, we have recently gone im for absentee insur-
ance. Through the want of a little local enterprise and
patriotism—so common in other lands—our best, werkers
are actually forced to feed the already over-gorged
absentee glutton.

An insurance company might commence to do busi-
ness, and continue working in Ireland without troubling
about the £20,000 Government deposit. There is, at
least, one company doing so in Ireland at present; it
issues policies promising certain payments at a fixed
future date, but accepts no risk on any person’s life—
in other words, it is an insurance company that would
not insure. Parliament set out in the carly seventies
with the laudable intention of preventing insurers from
being coaxed into doing business with unsubstantial
companies which speculative people might start with an
office, a large brass-plate, but not much funds. It
failed, however, to recognise what any insurance man
could have pointed out, and the net result is, a company

. can start without any funds provided it confines itself

to pure endowment business. OF course, it is possible
for a company, having much less funds than £20,000, o
be perfectly solvent. Solvency does not depend on big-
ness, but rather upon assets in relation to liability, One
of the most solvent companies known to the writer is
the smallest; it is in this unique position—if all its
risks “ fell in’” on ono day, it could pay 24s. in the £,
but is it not absurd for Parliament to insist that an
insurance company doing one kind of business shall pos-
sess £20,000 when commencing, and, at the same time,
not care apparently whether another issuing a different
sort of policy owns 20,000 farthings. 3

The stupidity of the law regarding this deposit will
be further appreciated when I say that American and
Colonial insurance companies have been known to be
given back their deposits on satisfying the Court of
Chancery that they “have sufficient funds.”” The ques-
tion, “ Where are they invested?’’ has not been asked,
and hence people may still be insured in the British
Isles in companies whose funds (or any part thereof)
are not invested any nearer home than is Timbuctoo
from Dublin. Supposing one of these concerns were dis-
honest enough to deny its just liability when a claim,
through one of its policies, arises, what redress would
the claimant have? Well, I daresay he could “go to
Timbuctoo.”” The law, as we find it, would certainly
do nothing for him.

Were we to have our own Parliament in Ireland to-
morrow we would still need our own Insurance Co. Eng-
land and Scotland have about eighty, Ireland has none,
and never had a mutual one. Irishmen of all parties,
especially those who believe in the policy of Sinn Fein
(or Treland for the Irish) should see to it without more
delay.

We can at least start with the right sort of Co. The
past, as I have endeavoured to show, points to the
folly and danger of having anything to do with the
other sort. It is a coincidence, after what I wrote in
the LEADER recently, against establishing any stoek in-
surance concern, that a Reuter cable appears in the
daily papers of Friday last, stating that the Joint Legis-
lation Committee appointed to investigate American
life insurance methods, recommends the mutualisation
of all stock insurance companies. Well, we in Ireland
do not possess even a stock company to mutualise—the
Patriotic and the National (two small but sound offices
—at least in their Life departments) having joined the
array of foreign companies that, at present, exploit the
whole field of insurance in this country. But we, I re-
peat, can start on right lines—let us do that or nothing.

A mutual insurance company owned and controlled by
Irishmen would necessarily have its head offices here.
The right sort of men to act as directors or trustees (sore
of our best known, most respected and successful busi-
ness men) would, without any doubt, readily come for-
ward if they could be shown that the new company
would be enthusiastically supported by the country.
This might be done, to a great extent, by readers of the

FURTHER THOUGHTS ON EDUCATION.

‘N some. foreign countries many things are better con-
sidered than they are in these kingdoms. Certainly,
" in some places they do not speak of a Ministor of Edu-
«cation, but of the Minister of Public Instruction. Some
may think this a needless, if not a. meaningless, dis-
‘tinction ; yet it is not. If education means a “ drawing
out ’ of the powers of the mind, how can you educato
& man who has really no powers to be drawn out? Cer-
tainly many men have no powers of mind, the drawing
forth of which could fittingly be dignified with the name
of education. Whether these are a majority of the
race or not, one does not care hastily to say; they are
«certainly a considorable proportion of mankind, and they
may be found in all grades of tho social scale. Stupidity
and blankness of the wits are not indigenous to any one
class, but may be found in all.

It appears to me that men, taking them as they come,
fall into three classes, so far as mind is concerned. First
come those who have powers of mind, and ave therefore
educable; second, those who have merely intelligence
.and memory, and are just instructible; last, those who
have no powers of mind, practically no intelligence, de-
fective memory, and are therefore uninstructible. The
unit of the latter class may be summed up as the pre-
«destinate blockhead. He is not matter for pedagogic
handling at all. I do not know if there is much use in
discussing whether the second or the third of these
classes is the more numerous; I take it the second is.
Mere intelligence and memory are almost as common as
clay, especially in Ireland; the inveterate blockhead is
almost as rare as the man of exceptional powers. The
real trouble arises over a different matter, viz., how to
sort out the educable from the instructible. In Ireland
this problem is particularly difficult. Our people have
a very quick, ready intelligence, and much receptivity.
These, combined with a good memory, handled by skil-
ful instruction, may produce results so deceptively like
the products of better powers, that it is not always easy,
in early life, to distinguish which of their possessors
‘belongs to one class and which to the other—the edu-
«cable, or the merely instructible. Men have had won-
derfully brilliant college courses, whose brilliancy seemed
to end with their college course, and whose whole after-
lives seemed to be simply a carefully prepared barren-
ness. Tupper, the author of the Proverbial Philosophy,
is said to have made a better college course than Glad-
stone; yet, look at the subsequent careers of the two
men! One was four times Prime Minister of England ;
the other became an impotent literary fumbler—now
all but forgotten. Cases of this kind are not infrequent.
It is supposed that the examination for Fellowship in
‘Trinity College is exceptionally severe and difficult; how
often do we find the winner of the Fellowship doing any-
thing remarkable in all his subsequent career? Rarely,
indeed. I am not to be taken as arguing from this
that an University education is no use; I merely suggest
that its use may easily be exaggerated, above all, to
men of unusual powers. In the end, men of exceptional
powers have to educate themselves. Rather, life and
:the world educate them, and “ draw out ’’ their powers.
I should have liked, for instance, to have seen some
-ordinary professor of egonomics essaying to teach Glad-
:stone the principles of finance, or a professor of com-
position teaching him how to make a Ministerial state-
‘ment, or to introduce a Billl It seems that when
‘Charles Lamb was at school, the teacher of English com-
1position had much and unprofitable trouble with him,
for he could never get; the future essayist and humourist
0 compose to hig liking, He would finish up a re-
:monstrance with his unthriving pupil with the declara-
stion, “ That is how we composed themes when I was at
:school,”” leaving the youngster, no doubt, unhappy at
‘hig inability to master the same sovereign method, One
is reminded of some humarous lines of Kipling— 5

2K Th;zre are four-and-twenty ways of composing tribal

ays,

And every single one of them is right.’”

The meaning of which is that every original spirit goes

to work in his own way; te ask him to do other-

THE LEADER.

MarcH 3, 1906.

wise is to ask him to leave off being himself,
which is unnatural, and, in any, case, impossible,
if he be veally an original spirit. It may
be said that in this way the value of all instruction
could be denied, but that is not so. What we have to
remember is that instruction is merely. a preparation.
Till tho seil never so carefully, the yield will depend on
the soil itself after all, rather than on the tilling. Still,
since good tilling makes a difference, even to the best
soil, let the tilling be well done; but do not imagine
that even the best tilling will turn a Sahara into a
Golden Vein, or make poor land produce more than a
tithe of what good land will produce with ease.

Surely the real problem of “education’ is how to
handle the natural powers of those who are to be
taught?! Yet what I commonly find is that theorists
and writers on this great question seem to have in their
mind’s eye some generic type of youth, with whom any-
thing can be done if only he be handled according to
their particular recipe! To my mind a hardly more
profitable notion is that of a kind of caste system in edu-
cation—a system of educating men as “classes’’ or
““masses,” or according to their (imagined) probable
future employment. The three grades of mentality I
have spoken of occur in all social states, and to ignore
this fact is to ignore Nature herself—a thing which
may not safely be done.

In Ireland, backward country that it is, the need
for the best mind-training is too imperiously urgent to
be trifled with in favour of any system that will save
the faces of this or that social caste. We have a rank
superabundance of the merely intelligent or instructible,
and, I fear, a serious shortage of minds of deep draught
and real capacity. This only makes the need of true
education, of a drawing forth of the best minds, all the
more pressingly urgent. It is by and upon such minds
that a nation really advances. Napoleon’s dictum, that
an army marches on its stomach, may be true of an
army ; but to-day a nation must march on its mind, or
be given over to inferiority. Moreover, it must march
on its best minds, not on its average, newspaper-led
minds, and to draw out those best minds is Ireland’s
highest task to-day. TaAL.

According to old records Ballybough once formed part
of the private estate of James the Second. We wish to
goodness he had been King of Ballybough instead of
King of England.
lexander sighed for fresh worlds to conquer.  We
can fancy Mr. William Field heaving a “ windy suspira-
tion of forced breath ™ for more Boards to serve upon.

We hear that the Nutting Charitable Endowment
for Poor Papists is being availed of by some young
“ soldiers of the true faith.”” We would welcome fresh
particulars of this new development of the Super-ior
University.

Joo has appointed Mr. Balfour to be leader of the
Conservative Party, reserving to himself the modest
office of leader-appointer. Arthur has managed to ac-
quiesce in this arrangement, pending some way of “ dish-
ing’ Joe.  Meanwhile they are in a high state of
brotherly love.

We should suggest a new management of the High
Sheriff difficulty for Dublin in future. Have a High
Sheriff, whose duties should be ornamented, such as
wearing the chain and attending public functions, es-
pecially dinners; have also a Low Sheriff, whose duty it
should be to take any abuse that happened to be going,
and say he liked it.

People are wanting to know will Lord Ikerrin be a
good butter Tnspector. We hope so. What is wanted
really, though, is somebody who knows how to spot the
bad butter.

His lordship’s new duties will involve his residing in
England. Should we then speak of him as “a poor
exile of Erin ’? Consult the salary list.

The Bishop of Limerick thinks that if the English
adopt secular education they will be “a nation of
heathens in a generation.” Faith, we are afraid a tall
percentage of them are so already; certainly many of
them are so-so as Christians.

What do you think Viscount Ikerrin said when he
received his much debated appointed?  Butter late
than never.”” And he still lives!

In a casein the London Court of Appeal it was stated
that the Zrish T'imes has “ a large English circulation.”

LEADRLR. 29

Of cowrse; sure, isn’t it the organ of the English in
Ireland?

The Zrish T'imes is said to have been lifting matter out
of the Sunday 1'imes. Does any paper ever lift literary
matter out of the 7.7.7 Only when they are very hard
up, we should say.

Now that Lent is in, we suggest that it would be
more seemly of the Corporation not to hold any meetings
during that penitential season. We object to play-act-
ing during Lent, altogether.

The division on the Saunderson amendment to the
Address was pleasing to Mr. Puck. Only 88 M.P.'s
could be got to “view with alarm ’’ the Irish proposals
of the Government, as against over 400 who viewed
sameo proposals without alarm. Thus may it ever be!

Mr. P. notes as a strange—very strange—fact, that
the two “largest circulations’’ still maintain an icy
silence as to what their circulation is!

The “ Added Areas’’ are quite generously treated Ly
the Corporation of the “ Methrawpolis *’—in some ways,
certainly. They are allowed to keep their own mud,
the City Fathers showing no greedy desire to deprive
them of it. Again, their roads are not interfered with
—not even in the way of repairing.

The disputes and discussions as to the burial-place of
Robert Emmet are making Mr. Puck very uneasy, lest
after his own death there should be any similar uncer-
tainty as to his place of sepulture. In case he should
ever be hanged, he throws upon the Government the
onus of recording his last resting-place.

We note that Bung O’Hara, of Dorset St., published
an apology to Bung Jameson (John and Son), in that
he, Bung O’Hara, sold as Bung Jameson’s whiskey
liquors not manufactured by the latter Bung. Honour
among Bungs!

We cannot make any forecast as to how the weather
will behave on St. Patrick’s Day, but we suspect that
it will be a wet day if “the houses’’ are open.

St. Patrick’s Day falls on a Saturday this year.
Bonas’ Day falls on a Sunday—every week. Blackrock
is the Mecca of the true bona; Dollymeunt the deputy
Mecca. Puck.

Father Selley will deliver the next lecture in his course

There was a curious sort of an “ Irish ”’ concert held
at “ the Athenum,” Limerick, recently. It was held
under the auspices of the Sarsfield Fife and Drum Band.
Tt was advertised to be “an ideal Irish night,”” and e
was some Trish on the posters. Some of the items were
all right, but why should a man dressed up in stage
Trish costume sing some sort of a song about Mrs. Magee
and her mud cabin, and how visitors were treated to a
“sate’’ on a sop of straw in the corner where the pig
does lie near the bed. Mrs. Magee and her visitors it
appears fell to smashing one another’s skulls with shille-
laghs in the end of the song. That would scarcely be
an ideal night even for yahoos.  Another item was
‘“ McSorly’s Twins.”

An Industrial Development Association has be=n
formed in Newry. Apparently the matter has been
taken up by a body of very representative people, and a
public meeting has been held by way of endoxrsing the
formation of the new association. In the course of his
remarks Mr. J. B. Galvin, who acted as secretary to the
meeting, said:—1It was estimated that every year over
£200,000 worth of foreign-milled flour came into Newry
and district. The local mills, if supported, could supply
flour not only for Newry, but for the whole of Ulster,
and the local flour was better and cheaper than any
foreign flour. Out of some £26,000 worth of tobacco
consumed in the Newry district each year, as much as
£20,000 was foreign; out of £300 worth of pipes used
every year £250 was foreign; out of £2,000 worth of
snuff used each year about £1,000 worth is foreign.
About £900 worth of foreign matches were used eve
year in Newry ; about £450 worth of foreign blacking ;
£10,000 worth of foreign bacon ; about £8,000 worth of
foreign soaps and powders; about £1,000 worth of
foreign candles ; while the value of foreign clothing and
other commodities, they felt sure, amounted to several
thousands of pounds more.”” In face of that general
statement of affairs there is plenty of room for the work
of an Industrial Association in Newry.
